没有人会窃取想法，因为将想法变成现实依赖于经验、运气和长期专注的工作。
请看下面的想法。我已经给了你需要的一切和要采取的步骤，这样你就可以窃取我的想法了。尽管列出了所有步骤并编写了需求，但没有人会将其转化为产品或业务。
世界各地的人们都渴望旅行，但他们不知道可以去哪里，也不知道如何安全到达那里。关于你可以去哪里，不能去哪里，哪些国家付钱让你去，每天都有文章出来，但实际上找到所有你可以去的地方和去那里的规则仍然需要大量的研究。基本上，无论是在国内还是国外，要弄清楚哪些地方可以旅行，哪些地方不能旅行，需要付出很大的努力。
为了解决这个问题，我可以做一个冠状病毒旅行应用程序，它聚合了所有这些COVID旅行信息，这样用户就知道他们可以去哪里，以及如何安全到达那里。
然后我可以通过到旅游网站的会员链接将流量货币化，或者如果有足够的流量，我可以制作一个向酒店出售赞助的电子邮件列表，并将其货币化。
听起来是个好主意，对吧？我再走三步。我真的会给你一个计划，告诉你如何制作这个网络应用程序，雇佣自由职业者，营销这个应用程序，这样你就可以窃取并启动它，即使你以前从来没有做过任何类型的软件。
首先，你需要弄清楚你的用户是谁，他们需要什么功能，什么平台最适合他们。我们选择的是那些认为自己对COVID免疫的年轻人。为了让您更容易窃取信息，让我们选择一个Web应用程序，这意味着这些信息将出现在网站上。应用程序需要比网站多得多的设置和时间，而且Web应用程序更容易找到开发人员。
要定义整个应用程序并写出需求…，需要做很多艰苦的工作。幸运的是，我已经为你写好了。
接下来，您需要为设计者制定视觉规划。该计划包括应用程序线框、徽标和灵感。这是一个有三个视图的应用程序：浏览、搜索、详细的旅行视图，所以你只需要粗略地画出这三个屏幕，以帮助设计师了解你想要的是什么。你还需要给他一个你喜欢的Pinterest网页应用版块，这样他就会对你认为好看的东西有所了解。
已经觉得不知所措了吗？不要担心线框或Pinterest板。只要把我写的要求发给设计师，可能会花点时间，但可能会让你上手。
最后，您需要一个徽标。让我们称它为COVID旅行应用。访问Looka.com并创建一个您喜欢的徽标。您可以稍后将徽标截图给设计师。
你可能不知道如何做你需要做的一切来窃取这个想法。所以你可能想雇些帮手。你需要三个主要的人：收集数据的虚拟助理，使应用程序可用的设计人员，以及使应用程序成为现实的开发人员。
你需要知道哪些国家对美国、加拿大和英国公民开放，每个国家都有什么程序，允许什么类型的旅行，以及我在说明书中为你提供的一大堆其他信息。
在Fiverr上雇佣一名数据输入虚拟助理(10-20美元)，在谷歌表格上填写你需要的所有信息。告诉他们列三张单子：
美国人、加拿大人和英国人可以出国旅行的每个地方，以及任何需要隔离两周的要求。
告诉他们列出美国人口最多的15个城市和所有50个州的避难所就位编号
要求他们根据博客作者在线评论的最佳COVID安全反应，对所有在美国、加拿大和英国运营的航空公司进行排名。
当虚拟助理在做这件事的时候，雇佣设计师。你需要一个自称是网站或网页应用程序设计师的人。理想情况下，找一个使用Adobe XD或Figma的人，这样你就不需要购买任何新软件来查看设计。聘请你喜欢的具有良好审美观的人。
告诉设计师设计一个响应迅速的网页，其中包括桌面和移动设计。将您的徽标截图、线框图片、Pinterest Board链接和要求发送给他们。
现在轮到开发商了。我建议继续做Upwork，这需要更长的招聘时间，但质量并不像Fiverr那样是垃圾。如果开发人员表现不佳或反应迟钝，您最终会浪费大量时间。我推荐来自东欧的开发商，如波兰，乌克兰，俄罗斯，白俄罗斯等。包括在工作岗位上Upwork的设计和要求，以便开发人员谁申请可以看到确切的计划。
给开发人员发消息，询问关于规范的具体问题，以及他们是否对API有任何担忧。
雇佣他们，测试构建，然后获得最终实现并将其放在您自己的域中。
现在是让人们使用你偷来的应用程序的时候了。要做到这一点，首先要与朋友和家人一起在自己的社交媒体账户上发帖。然后开始在旅行子网站、黑客新闻上发帖，并在COVID旅游文章上发表评论。有一天，你可以联系一个寻找产品的猎人来发布这款网络应用程序。联系Twitter上的博客作者，看看他们是否有兴趣写关于你的应用程序。联系对旅行有影响的人，看看他们是否有兴趣使用这款应用，并在他们的平台上谈论它。
向网站添加电子邮件截图，这样您就可以每周向用户发送旅游目的地建议。然后开始与你的客流量很大的地区周围的酒店交谈，看看他们是否想在电子邮件时事通讯上购买广告。
我估计这个项目的总成本在600-800美元左右，这取决于你能把开发商从他要求的1200美元讨价还价到什么程度。
如果营销得当，你可能会把这变成每月四位数的被动收入流，当然，…。你不会的。你可能会认为这是太多的工作，或者告诉你自己，成千上万的其他人读了这篇文章，现在正在做这件事，这样做是为了保护你的自尊。…。尽管我们都知道没人动过手指头。
我可以向你保证，没有人会窃取这个想法。六个月后，当流行病还在肆虐的时候，它仍然会在这里，我仍然会在这里向那些新的企业家摇头，他们太害怕了，不敢分享和获得对他们超级神奇的想法的反馈。
note_en:
No one is stealing ideas because making them real relies on experience, luck, and focused work for a long time.
  Look at the idea below. I’ve given you everything you need and the steps to take so you can steal my idea. In spite of laying out all the steps and writing the requirements no one is going to turn it into a product or business.
  People all over the world are itching to travel, but they don’t know where they can go or how to get there safely. Articles are coming out every day about where you can or can’t go, which countries are paying you to visit them, but actually finding all the places you can go and the rules to get there still requires a lot of research. Basically, it’s a lot of effort to figure out where you can and can’t travel right now both domestically and abroad.
 To solve this problem, I could make coronavirus travel app that aggregates all this COVID travel info so users know where they can go and how to safely get there.
 Then I could monetize the traffic via affiliate links to travel sites or if there’s enough traffic I could make and monetize an email list selling sponsorship to hotels.
 Sounds like a good idea, right? I’ll go three steps further. I’ll literally give you the plan for how to make this web app, hire the freelancers, and market the app so you can steal and launch it even if you never made any type of software before.
   First, you’ll need to figure out who your users are, what features they would need, and what platform is best for them. We’re going with younger people who think they’re immune to COVID. To make it easier for you to steal, let’s choose a web app which just means this info will be on a website. Apps require a lot more setup and time than websites plus web apps are easier to find developers for.
 That’s a lot of hard work to define the whole app and write out requirements…Luckily I  already wrote it for you.
 Next, you’ll need to make the visual plan for the designer. This plan includes an app wireframe, logo, and inspiration. This is a three-view app: browse, search, detailed trip view so you just need to roughly draw out those three screens to help the designer understand what you want. You’ll also need to give him a  Pinterest board of web apps you like so he has some idea of what you think looks nice.
 Already feeling overwhelmed? Don’t worry about the wireframe or Pinterest board. Just send the designer the requirements I wrote, it might take a bit longer but might get you started.
 Finally, you’ll need a logo. Let’s call this COVID Travel App. Go to  Looka.com and create a logo you like. You can just screenshot the logo give the designer later.
   You probably don’t know how to do everything you’ll need to steal this idea. So you might want to hire some help. You’ll want three main people: virtual assistant to gather the data, designer to make the app usable, and a developer to make the app real.
  You’ll need to know which countries are open to USA, Canadian, and British citizens what processes each country has in place, what type of travel is permitted, and a bunch of other info I put for you in the specs.
 Go on  Fiverr and hire a data entry virtual assistant ($10-$20) to fill out a google sheet with all that info you need. Tell them to make three lists:
 Each place Americans, Canadians, and British people can travel internationally to as well as any requirements like needing to quarantine for two weeks.
 Tell them to make a list of shelter in place phase number of the top 15 most populous U.S. cities and all 50 states
 Ask them to rank all airlines that operate in U.S. Canada, and the UK by best COVID safety response as reviewed by bloggers online
  While the virtual assistant is working on that, hire the designer. You’re going to want someone that says they are a website or web app designer. Ideally, find someone using Adobe XD or Figma so you don’t need to buy any new software to view the designs. Hire someone with a good aesthetic you like
 Tell the designer to design a responsive web with the desktop and mobile designs included. Send them your logo screenshot, picture of your wireframe, link to the Pinterest Board, and the requirements.
  Now for the developer. I recommend going on  Upwork for this, it will take longer to hire, but the quality is not garbage like Fiverr tends to be. If the developer is bad or unresponsive you’ll end up wasting a lot of time. I recommend developers from East Europe like Poland, Ukraine, Russia, Belarus, etc. Include the designs and requirements in the job post on Upwork so the developer who apply can see the exact plan.
 Message the developers and ask specific questions about the specs and if they have any worries about the APIs.
 Hire them, test the builds, then get the final implementation and put it on your own domain.
   Now it’s time to get people using your stolen app. To do that start by posting on your own social media accounts with friends and family. Then begin posting on travel subreddits, hacker news, and commenting about it on COVID travel articles. Reach out to a hunter on product hunt to post the web app one day. Reach out to bloggers on Twitter to see if they’re interested in writing about your app. Reach out to travel influencers to see if they’d be interested in using the app and talking about it on their platforms.
 Add an email capture to the website so you can send users travel destination suggestions every week. Then start talking to hotels around the area where a lot of your traffic comes from to see if they want to buy ads on the email newsletter.
 I’d estimate the total cost for this project to be around $600-$800 depending on how well you can negotiate the developer down from the ~$1200 he’s asking for
 If marketed well you could probably turn this into a four-figure a month passive income stream but of course… you won’t. You probably think it’s too much work or protect your ego by telling yourself a thousand other people read this article and are doing this right now…even though we both know no one has lifted a finger.
 I can promise you, no one is going to steal this idea. It’ll still be here in six months when the pandemic is still raging on and I’ll still be here shaking my head at those new entrepreneurs too scared to share and get feedback on their super amazing idea.
title: 偷走我的商业创意-你不会的
title_en: Steal my business idea – you won't

这部电影也为我们滞留在家中的时光提供了一个理想的背景，因为它让你轻松地沉浸在新奥尔良这个独特的社区中。作为一个在这座城市生活了几年的人，我欣赏一部不仅以新月城为背景，而且真正在新月城进行制作的电影的可能性可能要高出20%。(这就是CSI：新奥尔良的节目，对吗？此外，(#LoganIsUnderrated)，经常旅行的观众会认出下九区的著名景点，或者好莱坞最著名的废弃主题公园，以及更多当地的上篮，比如有轨电车、蓝色自行车或超级穹顶的背景面孔。弗兰克的史蒂夫·格里森(Steve Gleason)球衣或罗宾(Robin)用锡安·威廉姆森(Zion Williamson)作为她的电话背景，简单地代表了上面的糖粉。
然而，“权力计划”成功的最大推动力是在银幕之外。马特森·汤姆林(Mattson Tomlin)的剧本节奏很好，这是第一次有特色的编剧，也就是说，这部电影开始了，并保持了活跃。也许像罗宾的说唱场面这样的东西是多余的，但它们一直受到欢迎，而且从未逗留过久。这是汤姆林作品的整体表现--没有太多漫无目的的场景咀嚼、冗长的人物独白或过度紧张的动作场面。也许一些观众会对一些感觉被贴在上面的道德感到恼火(阿特一直告诉罗宾要培养自己的力量，以便与当权者作斗争；弗兰克有很多与各种组织腐败作斗争的时刻)，或者一些关键的阐述时刻，但后者可能是为了帮助那些可能像更传统的Netflix电影一样观看这部电影的人，也就是当他们只注意了一半的时候。你不必过分眯着眼睛就能看出汤姆林最近是如何发现自己在“蝙蝠侠”中被迫承担编剧职责的。
“力量计划”归根结底是你过去每年夏天都能在影院可靠找到的电影类型：一部动作片，闪现出一点喜剧，由你想要花近两个小时陪伴的人主演，而且发生的事情足够多，足以让观众通过它可能不是超级复杂的情节。(我在这里根本没看手机，上面说的是2020年Netflix的一部电影。)。
即使在新冠肺炎肆虐电影业之前，制片厂和影院也变得不太可能创作和发行这样的电影。随着制作预算的增长，以及对高票房回报的需求增加，继续这种高额财务行为的方式肯定会成为常态：知名的特许经营权或IP，儿童电影，专门为当下的一线明星设计的车辆(福克斯曾经肯定有资格，但今天不是-我是唯一一个在2018年假期期间家人拖着他们去看性感的罗宾汉(Robin Hood)的人吗？)，或者更多吹嘘奥斯卡抱负或电影制作人的导演项目，这些项目要么吹嘘奥斯卡的抱负，要么是让评论界感到兴奋的电影制作人。其他影片也在努力制作和/或找到进入影院的途径。
因此，流媒体已经开始填补这一空白，尤其是Netflix在这方面做了大量的工作。从《探索报》、《周一发生了什么？》、《静音》到《三重边疆》、《拔牙》、《老卫士》，Netflix或许已经成为电影院夏季主食的可靠去处。
Project Power就属于这个血统。它既不会赢得奥斯卡奖，也不会抓住互联网时代精神，也不会在评论界掀起一场风暴。当我周五早上登录观看时，Netflix甚至没有在页面横幅的顶部显示Project Power。但是，如果做得好甚至做得充分，这些有能力的B级电影或爆米花电影可能会很有趣-权力项目肯定有资格。
note_en:
Remember movie theaters? Those things were fun—even in the dead heat of summer, you could snag a $10 ticket for two hours of air conditioning, varying degrees of thrills, and the option to overspend on popcorn and M&Ms. Perhaps even better, this situation encouraged out-of-your-comfort-zone choice. Only so many different films can play on a theater's limited number of screens at once, so instead of being paralyzed by endless selection screen scrolling, you and your cohort simply aimed for what sounded best and maybe enjoyed something you wouldn't have tried otherwise.
 Project Power, available this weekend on Netflix, stars Jamie Foxx, Joseph Gordon-Levitt, and Dominique Fishback. It screams "summer action movie, available for a few weekends in July." But in 2020, such things remain fantasies of the past. That's a shame for many, many reasons, but one of the tinier downsides of the film industry's current reality is that  Project Power is a good time many people will simply never see, maybe never even know exists.
  In a broad sense,  Project Power tells a classic tale of those in power conducting experiments on the rest of us. A seedy scientific startup called Teleios has created a drug that gives humans superpowers (the drug's plainly called Power). The drug sits firmly in the development stage, though. Users don't know what power they'll develop—the list of classics includes chameleon-like camouflage, super strength, regeneration abilities, etc.—but not all of 'em end up benefitting users.
 Despite the imperfections, this company has evidently gotten a little impatient. Instead of going through the proper (and slow) channels of standard scientific approval, Teleios solicits drug dealers in New Orleans to distribute their product throughout town for a more informal trial. The goals are two-fold: see the drug's shortcomings so Teleios can continue to improve it, and showcase its capabilities so Teleios can sign fat government contracts abroad.
 Unleashing superhero pills in an unsuspecting city comes with some consequences, though. For "good" cop Frank (Gordon-Levitt), he notices the NOPD handles any incident involving Power quite differently, deferring immediately to men in black suits. (“ We know what happened last time we were counting on guys in suits to save New Orleans," he bluntly tells the chief.) For high schooler Robin (Fishback), she suddenly sees an easy way to make a buck as her mother deals with diabetes and a lack of health insurance. And for military vet Art (Foxx), well, his daughter has been missing and he has reason to believe she's caught up in this whole mess.
 Things start to snowball, however, when Teleios invites a South American buying group to town and Art arrives on the scene that same week. Both he and Frank want to get to the people behind Power, and the only person they know of with any connection happens to be Robin. But as the three of them start to cross paths and learn of their common pursuits, more and more pill-popping employees of Teleios seem to be following their tracks with intentions of cleaning up anything standing in the way of better drugs and more contracts.
   If all that sounds familiar, it kind of is. The world will get more "superpowers are complicated" stories this month (maybe) with  The New Mutants currently scheduled on August 28 and Amazon's   The Boys returning soon after on September 4. And these days, ample "governments manipulating the public to sustain control" fantasies can be found in the dark corners of any social media platform. (Legitimate versions of that can be found in any newspaper with government beat reporters, of course: "In the real world power goes to where it always goes, to the people who already had it," as Art puts it.)
 But  Project Power works—and would make for a fun sitting at a  drive-in, on the couch, or in a theoretically risk-free theater—because of how competently it executes a known blueprint. That starts with the performances. It's been awhile since Fox or Gordon-Levitt had a hit film, but maybe their best work has always subtly been in the action genre, from  Django to   Baby Driver to  Looper to  Dark Knight Rises. Each manages fight sequences and daring vehicle chases/escapes with ease (JGL even had a bike  accident during filming, but you wouldn't know it). Fishback may be the most consistently charming of the three leads, though. Anytime Robin showcases  the growing ability behind  her rapping dreams, for instance, it's among the film's most enjoyable moments. Even if it's not totally relevant to the plot in the end, I appreciate a budding rapper with the range to reference Celine Dion one moment and Dwight Howard the next.
 The film also has an ideal setting for our stuck at-home times, since it lightly immerses you in the unique community of New Orleans. As someone who spent a handful of years living in the city, I'm probably 20 percent more likely to enjoy a film that's not only set there, but genuinely holds production in the Crescent City. (This is how  CSI: New Orleans stays on the air, right? Also,  #LoganIsUnderrated.) Well-traveled viewers will recognize notable spots in the Lower Ninth Ward or  the most famous abandoned theme park in Hollywood among more locality layups like the street car, Blue Bikes, or background visages of the Superdome. Frank's Steve Gleason jersey or Robin using Zion Williamson as her phone background simply represent the powdered sugar on top.
 The biggest driver of  Project Power's success, though, happens off-screen. For a first time feature writer, Mattson Tomlin's script is nicely paced, which is to say this film starts and stays brisk. Maybe things like Robin's rapping scenes are superfluous, but they're consistently welcomed and never overstay their welcome. That's indicative of Tomlin's work overall—there's just not a ton of aimless scenery chewing stuff, long character monologues, or overwrought action sequences. Maybe some viewers will be annoyed with a few morals that feel pasted on (Art keeps telling Robin to nurture her power in order to combat those in power; Frank has a lot of "this is my city" moments battling various organizational corruption) or with some key moments of exposition, but the latter may be intended to help folks who may watch this like a more traditional Netflix movie, aka while they're only half paying attention. You don't have to squint too hard to see how  Tomlin recently found himself thrust into screenwriting duties on  The Batman.
    Project Power is ultimately the type of movie you used to be able to reliably find each summer at the theater: an action film that flashes a little comedy, stars someone you want to spend almost two hours with, and has enough happening to keep audiences moving through its perhaps not super complex plot. (I didn't look at my phone at all here, which says something about a Netflix film in 2020.)
 Even pre- COVID-19 ravaging the movie business, studios and theaters became less likely to create and distribute films like this. As production budgets grew and heightened the need for a big box office return, sure fire ways to continue that high wire financial act become the norm: known franchises or  IP, kids movies, vehicles designed specifically for an A-lister of the moment (which Foxx definitely once qualified, but not today—am I the only one whose family dragged them to see  sexy  Robin Hood during the 2018 holiday season?), or more auteur-y projects that boast either Oscar aspirations or filmmakers that excite the critical community. Other things struggled to get made and/or find their way to theaters.
 And so, streaming has stepped up to fill the void, with Netflix particularly doing so in a big way. From   The Discovery,   What Happened To Monday?, and  Mute to  Triple Frontier,   Extraction, and   The Old Guard, Netflix has perhaps become  the reliable spot for what used to be a summer staple at the cineplex.
 Project Power falls into this lineage. It will neither win an Oscar, capture the Internet zeitgeist, nor take the critical community by storm. Netflix didn't even have  Project Power showcased in the top of the page banner when I logged in to watch it Friday morning. But these capable B-movies or popcorn flicks can be fun when well or even adequately done— Project Power certainly qualifies.
title: Netflix的Project Power，今年夏天你看电影不会有的最大乐趣
title_en: Netflix’s Project Power, the most fun you won’t have at the movies this summer
